Article.ArticleTitle: Risk of hypertension and heart failure linked to high-normal serum sodium and tonicity in general healthcare electronic medical records.

Abstract.AbstractText: Population ageing is fuelling an epidemic of age-related chronic diseases. Managing risk factors and lifestyle interventions have proven effective in disease prevention. Recent epidemiological studies have suggested poor hydration as a modifiable risk factor, linked to a higher risk of chronic diseases. We evaluate whether hydration-related markers, serum sodium and tonicity, can serve as risk factors for hypertension and heart failure informing hydration-focused interventions in general health care.

We analysed 407 187 adults (18-104 years) from 20-year electronic medical records from Israel's Leumit Healthcare Services. We included individuals without significant chronic diseases and water balance issues, having normal serum sodium (135-146 mmol/L). Cox proportional hazards models were used to assess the risk of developing hypertension and heart failure. Our analysis showed an increased risk of hypertension with higher serum sodium levels: a 13% rise at 140-142 mmol/L and 29% for levels above 143 mmol/L [hazard ratio (HR) 1.29, 95% confidence interval (CI): 1.25-1.33]. Tonicity over 287 mosmol/kg was associated with a 19% increased risk of hypertension (HR 1.19, 95% CI: 1.17-1.22). The risk of heart failure also increased, reaching 20% for sodium above 143 mmol/L (HR 1.20, 95% CI: 1.12-1.29) and 15% for tonicity above 289 mosmol/kg (HR 1.16, 95% CI: 1.08-1.21). Among current healthy Leumit members, 19% had serum sodium within the 143-146 mmol/L range and 39% were in the 140-142 mmol/L range.

A serum sodium and tonicity exceeding 140 mmol/L and 287 mosmol/kg are associated with increased risk of hypertension and heart failure in the general Israeli population. These thresholds could be used to identify individuals at risk, warranting additional clinical evaluations and guiding preventive intervention.

Leumit Health Services, HaShalom Clinic, Nazareth, Israel.
Leumit Start, Leumit Health Services, Tel Aviv, Israel.
Laboratory of Cardiovascular Regenerative Medicine, National Heart Lung and Blood Institute, NIH, 10 Center Drive, MSC 1454 Building 10-CRC, Room 5E-3256, Bethesda, MD 20892, USA.

Grant.Agency: Elie Wiesel Chair at Bar-Ilan University
Intramural Research Program of the National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: In a routine blood test, a serum sodium level of 140 mmol/L or higher is linked to an increased risk of developing hypertension and heart failure, even though this level is within the normal range of 135–146 mmol/L.We analysed medical records from ∼700 000 members of Leumit Healthcare Services, one of the four health plans within Israel’s universal healthcare system. Our study looked back up to 20 years and focused on 407 187 individuals who were healthy at the time of their first blood test, noting their sodium levels at that point. By tracking who later developed hypertension and heart failure, we found that individuals with a serum sodium level of 140 mmol/L or higher were more likely to be diagnosed with these conditions.The study suggests that individuals with serum sodium levels in the upper part of the normal range may benefit from evaluating their daily fluid and salt intake habits, as well as potential water losses, all of which can affect serum sodium levels. This could help develop strategies for preventive measures to reduce their risk of hypertension and heart failure.
OtherAbstract.CopyrightInformation: © The Author(s) 2025. Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of the European Society of Cardiology.
